claims.claim: Aggregate productivity statistics will show no clear AI-driven gains through end-2026 while firm-level performance concentration keeps growing
claims.grounding: BLS/OECD productivity statistics and PwC/NBER firm-level concentration measures exist and publish on known schedules
claims.confidence_rationale: Productivity stats lag adoption by construction; concentration evidence (PwC/NBER signals) already strong. Risk: late-2026 statistical revision.
claims.update_test: [tightened 2026-06-10 grounding audit] Two conjuncts, scored explicitly: (A) BLS annual US nonfarm labor productivity growth for 2026 stays at or below 2.2% AND no BLS/OECD release attributes a stated uptick to AI; (B) at least one published 2026/early-2027 firm-level study (PwC, NBER, or equivalent) reports widening AI-performance concentration. Both hold = confirmed; exactly one = partial; neither = disconfirmed. Resolve on the 2026 annual releases, by 2027-03-31.

evidence.note: NBER w34836 Yotzov, Barrero et al. (Firm Data on AI, 2026): 6000-executive survey across US, UK, Germany, Australia. 69 percent of firms actively use AI. Nine-in-ten executives report no impact of AI on employment or productivity over the last 3 years. Adoption is real; conversion to measured output is not. Direct firm-level confirmation of the AI Solow paradox at end-Q2 2026, supporting the directional claim that aggregate productivity stats will not show clear AI-driven gains through end-2026.

NBER w34984 (Atlanta Fed CFO Survey, ~750 corporate executives, NBER series posted June 2026) reports labour productivity gains positive but concentrated in high-skill services and finance, with adoption heterogeneity. Companion paper w34836 reports ~1.5 hours/week median usage. Both point to firm-level concentration without aggregate breakthrough as of mid-2026 — consistent with the bruno claim direction.

NBER w34984 (Baslandze et al., June 2026): survey of ~750 corporate executives finds labor productivity gains POSITIVE, varying by sector, concentrated in high-skill services and finance, with effects expected to strengthen in 2026. Productivity paradox noted (perceived > measured). Claim is about AGGREGATE statistics through end-2026 - paper does not yet show aggregate BLS/BEA confirmation but signals firm-level positive trajectory consistent with disconfirmation if it propagates.

NBER firm-data work circulated mid-June 2026 (w34984 series) reports AI-attributable aggregate US employment decline expected at less than 0.4 percent for 2026, with offsetting growth in technical roles inside adopting firms - the firm-vs-aggregate divergence the claim names. Bears on conjunct B (firm-level concentration) more than conjunct A (BLS productivity); BLS 2026 annual print is the dispositive read and remains 2027.

NBER w34995 (Mind the Gap) reports US labor productivity +0.5-1.3pp associated with worker AI adoption in 2026 with strongly heterogeneous distribution (concentrated in high-skill services/finance); NBER w34984 reports 42% firms view AI as too immature and 36% workforce untrained (year-end 2025). Firm-level heterogeneity matches claim; aggregate productivity signal remains diffuse consistent with 'no clear AI-driven gains through end-2026' compound reading. Concordant, not resolving.

claims.update_test: Final omnibus text vs 2024 obligations: count of softened or delayed frontier-relevant articles

evidence.note: May 7 2026 Council+Parliament political agreement on AI Omnibus: Annex III HRAIS obligations deferred Aug 2026 → Dec 2027; Annex I deferred Aug 2027 → Aug 2028.

The interpretive substrate is being installed in parallel with the perimeter shrinking - net direction on enforceable obligations for frontier deployers is still reduction by end-2026, but the per-obligation compliance cost rises. Signal: 2026-06-12-eu-ai-office-enforcement-substrate-bifurcation.md

EU AI Act omnibus deal confirms Annex III HRAIS obligations deferred from 2026-08-02 to 2027-12-02 (16-month deferral) and Annex I HRAIS from 2027-08-02 to 2028-08-02 (12-month deferral); SME-style simplifications extended to 750-employee/EUR150M firms. The omnibus is delivering the predicted reduction in enforceable obligations for frontier deployers.

Council, Parliament, Commission reached provisional political agreement on Digital AI Omnibus 7 May 2026; Council Member State representatives confirmed deal 13 May 2026. The omnibus deferred high-risk obligations to 2 Dec 2027 (Annex III) and 2 Aug 2028 (Annex I products) - reduces near-term enforceable compliance burden on frontier deployers. Formal vote expected June-July 2026, entry into force shortly after publication in OJ.

Digital Omnibus text approved by EP 2026-06-16 (423-57-174) defers Annex III HRAIS obligations from 2026-08-02 to 2027-12-02 (16 months); Annex I product-regulated HRAIS slip from 2027-08-02 to 2028-08-02; Member-State sandbox obligation from 2026-08-02 to 2027-08-02. The 2026-08-02 enforcement date for use-based high-risk systems effectively does not exist post-adoption. Direct support for the 'reduces enforceable obligations through end-2026' thesis.

EP-approved omnibus text (2026-06-16) defers Annex III high-risk standalone obligations from 2026-08-02 to 2027-12-02 (16 months) and Annex I product-regulated obligations from 2027-08-02 to 2028-08-02 (12 months); synthetic-content machine-readable marking postponed from 2026-08-02 to 2026-12-02. Multiple frontier-relevant articles softened or delayed before end-2026 deployment. Not yet final law — pending Council and OJ publication — but the count-of-softened-articles update test now reads supportive.

Council formal adoption 2026-06-29 (Omnibus VII) enshrines the simplification turn in law. Extends high-risk deadlines (2027-12/2028-08) but does not directly touch GPAI/frontier obligations, which continue from 2026-08-02 as scheduled. Directional support for the mechanism named in the claim (regulatory turn favouring deployers), but the current text targets high-risk not frontier specifically - interpretive bearing on frontier deployer relief. Defer for structural resolution.

EU AI Office confirms 2026-08-02 as the activation date for Commission enforcement powers over GPAI providers (Article 55 obligations remain from 2025-08-02). The May 2026 Digital Omnibus on AI provisional agreement reinforced the AI Office's central supervisory role but 'did not change the GPAI regime' — meaning the omnibus turn has kept the Article 55 obligations formally in place. Fresh reading: legal-compliance industry primers dated 2026-07 confirm the enforcement powers arrive as planned; this bears IN FAVOR of claim-20260610-004 continuing to hold that enforceable obligations for frontier deployers will NOT be materially reduced before end-2026 as originally scoped.

claims.claim: Alphabet Q2 2026 earnings release (scheduled 2026-07-22) reports Google Cloud segment year-over-year revenue growth at or above 60 percent.
claims.grounding: Alphabet Q1 2026 Google Cloud revenue printed 20.0 billion USD up 63 percent YoY with sequentially near-doubled 462 billion USD backlog signalling acceleration; Q2 2026 earnings release is scheduled 2026-07-22 after market close per Alphabet investor relations calendar.
claims.confidence_rationale: Twin 65 pct vs base 50 pct. The 60 percent threshold is 3 pp below Q1 63 percent - a modest deceleration would still clear it, while backlog nearly doubling sequentially argues for acceleration rather than deceleration. Cloud has outpaced hyperscaler peers (Azure ~31 pct) and demand has not saturated; capex reallocation guidance implies management sees another year of growth. Downside path is a supply-constrained print similar to Azure's stated capacity limits, plausible but not majority. 50-59 pct bucket correction 0.009 and 60-69 pct 0.017 (calibration 2026-07-17) leave the corrected estimate essentially unchanged.
claims.update_test: Read Alphabet Q2 2026 earnings press release, 10-Q, or earnings-call slide deck published on 2026-07-22 or 2026-07-23. Confirmed if the disclosed Google Cloud segment revenue year-over-year growth rate is 60.0 percent or above; disconfirmed if below 60.0 percent.

prereg_audit.note: Searched Alphabet investor relations, IG, InsiderFinance, MarketBeat, BigGo Finance, Yahoo Finance analyst-preview coverage 2026-07-18: nothing decisive published yet. Analyst previews range from ~55 to 70 percent; BofA 70 percent, IG cites 63 percent as Q1 anchor, others do not commit to a Q2 point estimate. No liquid prediction-market price located for the 60 percent threshold.

claims.claim: Meta Platforms Q2 2026 earnings release (scheduled 2026-07-29) raises full-year 2026 capital expenditure guidance ceiling above 145 billion USD.
claims.grounding: Meta current full-year 2026 capex guidance is 125-145 billion USD, raised at Q1 2026 earnings from the prior 115-135 billion USD range. Q2 2026 earnings release scheduled 2026-07-29 after market close. Yahoo/StockTitan/Finance-Calendar confirm date; Seeking Alpha CapEx-and-Free-Cash-Flow-in-focus preview and TradingKey Meta Compute launch coverage set expectations for further capacity commitments.
claims.confidence_rationale: Twin 50 pct vs base 40 pct. Meta already raised guidance once this year (115-135 to 125-145 at Q1), so the base rate for a second raise in one year is materially below coin-flip; but Meta pattern under Zuckerberg is aggressive AI capacity commitment, and the July TradingKey coverage of the September Iris chip launch and Meta Compute rollout suggests further capacity build-out is being signalled ahead of the earnings call. The offsetting force is that management typically avoids raising a range twice in one year to preserve credibility. 50-59 pct bucket correction 0.009 (calibration 2026-07-17) leaves the corrected estimate at approximately 51 pct.
claims.update_test: Read Meta Q2 2026 earnings press release, 10-Q, and CFO commentary on the earnings call published 2026-07-29 or 2026-07-30. Confirmed if any of these documents state a full-year 2026 capex range whose upper bound (ceiling) is strictly greater than 145 billion USD; disconfirmed if the guidance ceiling is 145 billion USD or lower or if guidance is unchanged from the 125-145 billion USD range.

prereg_audit.note: Searched Yahoo Finance, Finance Calendar, StockTitan, Seeking Alpha, TradingKey, Simply Wall St analyst coverage 2026-07-18: Q2 release is 2026-07-29, no capex guidance ceiling revision has been pre-announced. claims.grounding: WAICO founded 2026-07-16 in Shanghai with 29 country founding signatories per Xinhua English (english.news.cn/20260716/2d364f232dd7456c8edf1d67d5125d37/c.html) and Wikipedia; open-membership design confirmed by Wang Yi statements and UN Secretary-General attendance; Ethiopia already reported joining as a founding member and additional Global South states have expressed interest per Antara Indonesia coverage.
claims.confidence_rationale: Raw 0.60 based on China's post-founding recruitment pattern in comparable multilateral organizations (SCO added 3 members within 2 years of founding; BRI cooperation initiatives added members steadily post-launch) and the explicit open-membership design. Applied calibration 2026-07-18 bucket 60-69 correction +0.017 to reach 0.617, rounded to 0.62. Base rate 0.45 estimates the naive prior for adding 4+ member states in 60 days given typical multilateral organization recruitment timelines. The 60-day window is short - the honest edge is small - and the outcome depends on whether China schedules a signing ceremony or public accession before mid-September.
